name: HMS Bullen (K 469)
events: 22 May 1944 HMS Safari (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J.R.G. Harvey, RN) conducted attack exercises for the C.O.Q.C. (Commanding Officers Qualifying Course) in the Clyde area during which HMS Braithwaite (Lt.Cdr. E.M. Mackay, RD, RNR) and HMS Bullen (Lt. A.H. Parish, RN) served as the targets. These included night exercises. (1)

23 May 1944 HMS Safari (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J.R.G. Harvey, RN) conducted night attack exercises for the C.O.Q.C. (Commanding Officers Qualifying Course) in the Clyde area during which HMS Braithwaite (Lt.Cdr. E.M. Mackay, RD, RNR) and HMS Bullen (Lt. A.H. Parish, RN) served as the targets. Upon completion of these exercises HMS Safari returned to Rothesay. (1)

24 May 1944 HMS Safari (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J.R.G. Harvey, RN) conducted attack exercises for the C.O.Q.C. (Commanding Officers Qualifying Course) in the Clyde area during which HMS Braithwaite (Lt.Cdr. E.M. Mackay, RD, RNR), HMS Bullen (Lt. A.H. Parish, RN) and HMS Jan van Gelder (Lt. P.D. O'Driscoll, RNR) served as the targets. These included night exercises. (1)

25 May 1944 HMS Safari (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J.R.G. Harvey, RN) conducted night attack exercises for the C.O.Q.C. (Commanding Officers Qualifying Course) in the Clyde area during which HMS Braithwaite (Lt.Cdr. E.M. Mackay, RD, RNR), HMS Bullen (Lt. A.H. Parish, RN) and HMS Jan van Gelder (Lt. P.D. O'Driscoll, RNR) served as the targets. Upon completion of these exercises HMS Safari proceeded to Rothesay. (1)

6 Jun 1944 Around 0530 B/6, HMS Nelson (Capt. A.H. Maxwell-Hyslop, AM, RN) departed Milford Haven for Plymouth where she arrived around 1930 B/6. She was escorted by the destroyer HMS Duncan (Lt. D.G.D. Hall-Wright, RN) and the frigates HMS Goodall (Lt.Cdr. J.V. Fulton, RNVR) and HMS Bullen (Lt. A.H. Parish, RN). (2)
This convoy departed Freetown around 0800 Z/9.

On departure from Freetown the convoy was made up of the following merchant vessels; Allerton (British, 7195 GRT, built 1941), Appledore (British, 5218 GRT, built 1929), Argyll (British, 4897 GRT, built 1939), Baron Elgin (British, 3942 GRT, built 1933), Charles Schiaffino (French, 3664 GRT, built 1930), Clunepark (British, 3491 GRT, built 1928), Domby (British, 5582 GRT, built 1932), Empire Beauty (British, 7297 GRT, built 1943), Empire Guinevere (British, 7072 GRT, built 1942), Fort Glenlyon (British, 7132 GRT, built 1943), Fort Thompson (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), Fort Wellington (British, 7131 GRT, built 1943), Hoggar (French, 5146 GRT, built 1923), Nailsea Moor (British, 4926 GRT, built 1937), Ocean Vanity (British, 7174 GRT, built 1942), Palembang (Dutch, 7070 GRT, built 1921), Port Darwin (British, 8063 GRT, built 1918), Samdart (British, 7219 GRT, built 1944), Samderwent (British, 7219 GRT, built 1944), Sansu (British, 5446 GRT, built 1939) and Sydland (Swedish, 5134 GRT, built 1920).

On departure from Freetown the convoy was escorted by the frigates HMS Odzani (Lt.Cdr. L.A. Sayers, RD, RNR), HMS Aire (A/Lt.Cdr. N.K. Boyd, DSC and Bar, RNR) and the patrol vessel HMS Kilmington (T/Lt. D.D. Humphreys, RNVR).

At 1100 Z/12, in position 14°15'N, 17°51'W, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Dakar; Bactria (British, 2407 GRT, built 1928), Empire Duchess (British, 7067 GRT, built 1943) and Oued Fes (French, 2554 GRT, built 1913).

At 1000 Z/13, in position 17°33'N, 17°55'W, the Empire Guinevere parted company with the convoy to proceed to Dakar due to engine trouble. HMS Kilmington escorted her to Dakar and then rejoined the convoy.

At 1700 Z/19, in position 33°56'N, 10°30'W, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Casablanca; Dagrun (Norwegian, 4562 GRT, built 1928) and Empire Planet (British, 4290 GRT, built 1923). They had been escorted to the rendezvous by the patrol vessels L'Emporté and L'Enjoue which subsequently returned to Dakar taking the merchant vessels Charles Schiaffino, Clunepark, Hoggar and Oued Fes with them. They arrived at Casablanca on 20 August.

At 0700 Z/20, in position 35°08'N, 10°16'W, the current escort of the convoy, HMS Odzani, HMS Aire and HMS Kilmington parted company to proceed to Gibraltar. They took the merchant vessel Empire Duchess with them. They arrived at Gibraltar on 21 August.

At 0800 Z/20, convoy MKS 58(G) joined and merged with convoy SL 166 in position 35°08'N, 10°06'W. The following merchant vessels now joined the combined convoy SL 167 / MKS 58(G); Astrid (Norwegian, 2861 GRT, built 1942), Benedick (British (tanker serving as escort oiler), 6978 GRT, built 1928), Boreas (Norwegian, 2801 GRT, built 1920), Capitaine Paul Lemerle (French, 4945 GRT, built 1925), Lagarto (British, 5072 GRT, built 1917), Nolisement (British, 5084 GRT, built 1928), Samshee (British, 7210 GRT, built 1944), Samthar (British, 7176 GRT, built 1943) and Van de Velde (Dutch, 6389 GRT, built 1919). Also joining were the submarines HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC and Bar, RN) for onward passage to the U.K.

The escort of concoy MKS 58(G) now formed the escort of the combined convoy SL 167 / MKS 58(G). This escort was made up of the following warships; frigates HMS Bullen (Lt.Cdr. A.H. Parish, RN), HMS Antigua (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) D.F. White, RNR) and the corvettes HMS Allington Castle (T/A/Lt.Cdr. W.G. Ogden, DSC, RNVR), HMS Hurst Castle (Lt. H.G. Chesterman, DSC and Bar, RNR), HMS Knaresborough Castle (Lt. J.R. Freeman, RNR), HMS Leeds Castle (T/A/Lt.Cdr. W.T. Hodson, DSC, RNVR) and HMS Tintagel Castle (Cdr. G.A. Thring, DSO, RN).
At 1500 Z/20, in position 35°45'N, 11°16'W, the rescue ship Copeland (British, 1526 GRT, built 1923) joined the convoy.

At 1400 A/21, in position 37°11'N, 13°50'W, the escort carrier HMS Activity (Capt. C. Wauchope, DSC, RN) joined the convoy coming from convoy OS 86 / KMS 60(G). She was escorted to the rendezvous with convoy SL 167 / MKS 58(G) by HMS Allington Castle and HMS Leeds Castle which had been detached earlier for this purpose.
At 1730 Z/22, in position 39°49'N, 15°48'W, the merchant vessel Samshire (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943) joined the convoy coming from the Azores.
At 1030 B/27, in position 50°07'N, 05°54'W, the Benedick was detached to convoy OS 87 / KMS 61(G).
At 2215 B/27, in position 51°26'N, 04°30'W, HMS Activity was detached to proceed to Greenock. HMS Antigua went with her as escort.
At 2222 B/27, in position 51°26'N, 04°30'W, the following merchant vessels were detached to Barry Roads; Baron Elgin and Boreas.
At 2258 B/27, in position 51°26'N, 04°41'W, the Nailsea Moor (bound for Loch Ewe for onward routing to a east coast port) was detached to Barry Roads with engine trouble.

At 2000 B/28, in position 53°27'N, 04°48'W, the Mersey and Morecambe Bay sections of the convoy parted company. This were the following merchant vessels; Argyll, Bactria, Empire Beauty, Fort Wellington, Nolisement, Ocean Vanity, Palengbang, Samdart, Samshee, Samshire, Sansu and Sydland.
At 2100 B/28, in position 53°33'N, 04°49'W, HMS Ultimatum and HMS Ultor parted company to proceed to Rothesay and Holy Loch respectively. HMS Bullen went with them as escort.

At 0930 B/29, in position 54°57'N, 05°09'W, the merchant vessel Empire Planet was detached to the Clyde, as was the rescue ship Copeland. The Lagarto (bound for Loch Ewe for onward routing to a east coast port) was also detached to the Clyde due to engine trouble.
The remaining ships of the convoy proceeded to Loch Ewe. (3)
The remaining merchant vessels of convoy MKS 58 passed Gibraltar into the Atlantic in the evening of 18 August 1944.

This were the following merchant vessels; Capitaine Paul Lemerle (French, 4945 GRT, built 1925), Lagarto (British, 5072 GRT, built 1917), Samshee (British, 7210 GRT, built 1944), Samthar (British, 7176 GRT, built 1943) and Van de Velde (Dutch, 6389 GRT, built 1919).

The new escort for convoy MKS 58(G) had joined the convoy at 2000 A/18 relieving the Mediterranean escort which then parted company to proceed to Gibraltar. The new escort was made up of the following vessels; Frigates HMS Bullen (Lt.Cdr. A.H. Parish, RN), HMS Antigua (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) D.F. White, RNR) and the corvettes HMS Allington Castle (T/A/Lt.Cdr. W.G. Ogden, DSC, RNVR), HMS Hurst Castle (Lt. H.G. Chesterman, DSC and Bar, RNR), HMS Knaresborough Castle (Lt. J.R. Freeman, RNR), HMS Leeds Castle (T/A/Lt.Cdr. W.T. Hodson, DSC, RNVR) and HMS Tintagel Castle (Lt. R. Atkinson, DSC and Bar, RNR).

With these new escort vessels the following merchant vessels also joined the convoy; Astrid (Norwegian, 2861 GRT, built 1942), Benedick (British (tanker serving as escort oiler), 6978 GRT, built 1928), Boreas (Norwegian, 2801 GRT, built 1920) and Nolisement (British, 5084 GRT, built 1928). The submarines HMS Ultimatum (Lt. W.H. Kett, RNR) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC and Bar, RN) also joined the convoy for onward passage to the U.K.
At 0800 Z/20, the convoy made rendezvous with convoy SL 167 coming from Freetown and the combined convoy SL 167 / MKS 58(G) was formed. (4)
